communications.subject: News Release: DCYF, Counties Reach Agreement to Lift Intake Freeze at JR Facilities
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3adb0f7 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Aug. 9, 2024

 
 

*DCYF, Counties Reach Agreement to Lift Intake Freeze*

*at JR Facilities*

“We have reached an agreement with our county partners that will lift the intake freeze at our Juvenile Rehabilitation facilities. We resumed intakes to our Echo Glen facility a few weeks ago and will resume intakes to Green Hill School on Aug. 9."

"We appreciate the counties’ engagement on this difficult situation. Because the population at Green Hill remains significantly above capacity, our focus will continue to be the safety of staff and young people at our facility."

"We are working with legislators, counties and stakeholders on the three actions necessary for ensuring all the young people in JR can safely access the programming and services they need – opening new space, recruiting and retaining skilled workers, and receiving data from the counties so we can be more responsive to changes in the JR population.”

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: Washington Reduces the Number of Children in Out-Of-Home Care by Nearly Half
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3ae9915 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Aug. 14, 2024

 
 

Washington Reduces the Number of Children in Out-Of-Home Care by Nearly Half

*Olympia, WA — *The number of children in out-of-home care is now only about half of what it was when the Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) was created.

There are now 4,971 children and youth in out-of-home care in Washington State. This is the lowest it’s been since the 80s and a sharp contrast from the 9,171 it was in 2018.

“Outcomes like this demonstrate our agency’s commitment to keeping families together and children and youth safe,” said DCYF Secretary Ross Hunter. “Although the number of reports we are receiving remain consistent, we are seeing fewer children and youth in out-of-home care as families are being referred to support services rather than having children removed from their homes.”

DCYF continues to see the same number of child abuse and neglect reports that require face-to-face responses and has received the same number of calls — nearly 92,000 calls to report suspected child abuse or neglect were made in 2023, compared to a little over 93,000 in 2018.

However, DCYF has changed how the state serves families. For the last couple of years, the agency has worked on initiatives such as Thriving Families [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/practice/practice-improvement/thriving-families ] that focus on preventing out-of-home placements, reducing racial disproportionality, and supporting families so children and youth remain safely at home.

DCYF continues to invest in strengths-based, wraparound prevention services to support families prior to child welfare intervention as well as improving its caregiver licensing process and supports especially for kin as the agency and the communities it serves will always have a need for kinship caregivers and foster parents.

"We know that supporting and collaborating with families by providing access to services and programs increases their number of protective factors, leading to better outcomes,” said DCYF Assistant Secretary Natalie Green. “Giving families the tools they need to thrive and safely parent means more children and youth remain safely at home."

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Celebrates Kinship Month, Improves Supports for Kin
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3b6e23f ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Sept. 23, 2024

 
 

DCYF Celebrates Kinship Month, Improves Supports for Kin

*Olympia, WA —* More than half of the children and youth in foster care are being placed with relatives in Washington State as the Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) continues to embrace a kin-first culture.

This month, DCYF joins the nation to celebrate Kinship Month, a time dedicated to recognizing relatives and suitable others who provide a safe and loving home to children in out-of-home care.

Research shows that placing children and youth with kin not only reduces trauma but is significantly better for their behavioral and mental health long-term.

Over the last six years, DCYF increased the number of children placed with kinship caregivers from 46% to 57%. Additionally, the number of kinship caregivers that were licensed and receiving supports increased from 228 to 1,836.

Licensing caregivers is significant to addressing existing inequities because it allows agencies to connect caregivers with important resources like financial assistance.

DCYF’s Kinship Standards [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/foster-parenting/kinship-caregivers/licensing-process/licensing-standards ] project was established to make it easier for kin to get licensed and access critical resources for caring for children and youth. The goal for the agency and the project is to have 80% of kinship caregivers licensed by the end of 2025, including those who care for Tribal children.

Some of the improvements made to better support kinship caregivers include:


  * Issuing Initial Licenses for kinship caregivers beginning July 2023, allowing kin to begin to receive foster care maintenance payments. 
  * Launching the Kinship Connection newsletter [ https://content.govdelivery.com/bulletins/gd/WADEL-3afd2dc?wgt_ref=WADEL_WIDGET_3 ] in June 2024. 
  * Creating New Kinship WACs [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/foster-parenting/kinship-caregivers/licensing-process/licensing-standards ] available for public review in December 2024. 
  * Updating reimbursement levels for all licensed caregivers as of January 2024. 
  * Launching a new, streamlined Kinship Home Study Update later this month. 

“DCYF wants to make sure that these caregivers are supported all year round,” explained DCYF Assistant Secretary of Licensing Ruben Reeves. “The moves DCYF is making now are allowing kinship caregivers to be properly compensated and I can’t wait to see how things improve as we continue to streamline our processes and increase supports.”

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Awards Nearly $3.7M to Early Learning Programs Serving Tribal Children
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3b81e7d ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Sept. 26, 2024

 
 

*DCYF Awards Nearly $3.7M to Early Learning Programs Serving Tribal Children*

*Olympia, WA — *Over 30 tribal-serving organizations, including 13 tribal nations, are receiving nearly $3.7 million in funds from the Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) to provide early learning, child care, prenatal, and home visiting programs to tribal children.

The awardees include 34 tribes, tribal providers, and tribal-serving organizations, for a requested total of $3,698,869 in funds. This is the second round of Tribal Early Learning Funds (TELF) provided by DCYF in 2024; a previous round of grant funding [ https://content.govdelivery.com/bulletins/gd/WADEL-3942cb1?wgt_ref=WADEL_WIDGET_277 ] was distributed in April for a total of $2.4 million. 

Tribes receiving funding include:


  * Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation 
  * Lower Elwha Klallam Tribe 
  * Nisqually Indian Tribe 
  * Nooksack Indian Tribe 
  * Port Gamble S’Klallam Tribe 
  * Puyallup Tribe 
  * Sauk-Suiattle Indian Tribe 
  * Shoalwater Bay Indian Tribe 
  * Spokane Tribe of Indians 
  * Squaxin Island Tribe 
  * Stillaguamish Tribe of Indians 
  * Suquamish Tribe 
  * Tulalip Tribes 

These funds will serve an estimated 1,671 students and tribal families across Washington state.

TELF is designed to provide resources to ensure early learning programs can best serve tribal children, ages prenatal to five, in an inclusive and culturally appropriate manner. Priorities are centered around language development, elder storytelling, outdoor-based learning, and incorporating traditional foods, music, and art in the classroom.

“These funds are being used in so many creative ways by our tribal-serving partners,” said Tleena Ives, DCYF Director of Tribal Relations. “We are incredibly excited to see the ways that these funds have helped to grow and foster ancestorial ties between tribes and their youngest learners.”  

For information about TELF, contact the Office of Tribal Relations at dcyf.tribalrelations@dcyf.wa.gov.

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Accepting Nominations for 2025 Unsung Hero Awards
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3bbfb60 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Oct. 15, 2024

 
 

DCYF Accepting Nominations for 2025 Unsung Hero Awards

*Olympia, WA *– The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) is currently accepting nominations for outstanding parents, grandparents, kinship caregivers, foster parents, and community members, providing direct care to children, as part of its annual Unsung Hero Awards.

For the past 13 years, DCYF has recognized caregivers who exemplify one or more of the five protective factors known to help reduce child abuse and neglect:


  * *Parental Resilience – *demonstrates the ability to bounce back from stress. 
  * *Social Connections – *demonstrates access to a strong support system to help weather challenges during hard times. 
  * *Knowledge of Parenting and Child Development – *demonstrates an understanding of a child’s developmental needs and the parenting strategies to support a child’s physical, cognitive, language, social, and emotional development. 
  * *Social and Emotional Competence of Children – *demonstrates empathy and support toward a child’s social and emotional wellbeing. 
  * *Concrete Supports in Times of Need* – demonstrates an awareness of where to access essential items and resources to minimize and avoid crisis. 

Twenty-eight nominees from across Washington state will be selected and recognized at a Parent Recognition Month banquet in February.

To nominate, individuals can complete the form [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/forms?field_number_value=02-026&title ] and email their nomination to strengtheningfamilies@dcyf.wa.gov. *The nomination deadline is Jan. 3, 2025.*

“It is encouraging each year to read about those that are nominated and hear about their unique and remarkable stories,” said DCYF Secretary Ross Hunter. “Caregivers are often not recognized for the work they put in day after day. I’m proud that we take some time to recognize these unsung heroes.”

Visit DCYF’s Strengthening Families Washington [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/child-development-supports/sfwa ] webpage to learn more about the five protective factors.

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: Washington Marks its 20th Year Celebrating Adoption Day
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3c2ccb9 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Nov. 20, 2024

 
 

Washington Marks its 20th Year Celebrating Adoption Day

*Olympia, WA *– Dozens of children and youth will be adopted in ceremonies across Washington Nov. 21-23 as the state joins the nation to celebrate Adoption Day.

Every year, the Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) joins the Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC) in celebrating Adoption Day to raise awareness around the continued need for adoptive families.

Gov. Jay Inslee proclaimed Nov. 23, as Adoption Day [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2024/11/19/file_attachments/3076000/Adoption%20Day%202024%20Proclamation.pdf ] in Washington to acknowledge the critical and significant commitment individuals make when they welcome a child or youth into their family.

At least 15 courts across the state plan celebrations, many of which will include finalizing adoptions. These events are open to the public for those who want to celebrate and take part. To find out if your county is celebrating, visit the list of events at Washington State Courts - National Adoption Day. [ https://www.courts.wa.gov/newsinfo/adoptionDay/?fa=adoptionDay.events ]

“We are so grateful for the families who are willing to open their hearts and homes for the children and youth in our care — when that placement becomes permanent, it’s really special,” said Ross Hunter, Secretary for DCYF. “Thank you also to the DCYF staff who work hard to create compatible placements, without them this would not be possible.”

Some of the extra special ceremonies this year, include three siblings being adopted together and a child that has found his forever home after spending nearly three years in foster care.

Since Jan. 1, 2024, more than 550 children and youth have been adopted in Washington state. Currently, more than 800 children in the Washington foster care system are legally free to be adopted into new families.

Washington’s statewide celebration is sponsored by the Washington Supreme Court Commission on Children in Foster Care, a joint commission of the Supreme Court and DCYF, supported by the AOC.

To learn more about adopting in Washington State and resources available to adoptive families, visit https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption. [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption ]

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator, DCYF | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Enters into Landmark Agreement with Tlingit & Haida Indian Tribes of Alaska
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3c5f97a ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Dec. 9, 2024

 
 

*DCYF Enters into Landmark Agreement with Tlingit & Haida Indian Tribes of Alaska *
tlingit & haida ceremony

Photo courtesy of Tlingit & Haida. Pictured: DCYF Secretary Ross Hunter (left) and Tlingit & Haida President Chalyee Éesh Richard Peterson (right).

*Olympia, WA *– The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) and the Central Council of the Tlingit & Haida Indian Tribes of Alaska (Tlingit & Haida) have entered into a historic agreement that will provide support for child protective services, foster care, dependency guardianship, termination of parental rights, and adoption proceedings.

The agreement was signed at a ceremony held on Dec. 4 in Juneau, Alaska.

This is the first formal partnership between DCYF and an out-of-state tribe, marking a groundbreaking step in intergovernmental collaboration. The agreement clarifies roles and responsibilities to enhance coordination and cooperation between the Tribe and DCYF in providing Indian Child Welfare Act (ICWA) services.

“This agreement sets a precedent, reinforcing the importance of ICWA principles and creating a model for collaboration across state boundaries,” said DCYF Secretary Ross Hunter. “We look to Tlingit & Haida as a model and hope they inspire other tribes to partner with other states.”

Nearly 23% of Tlingit & Haida tribal citizens under the age of 18 reside in Washington state — approximately 1,600 children and youth. Families will receive services from the Tribe’s Family Services Division based out of their recently opened Lynnwood office.

Information about DCYF’s ICWA division can be found at www.dcyf.wa.gov/tribal-relations/icw [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/tribal-relations/icw ].  

###

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Updates Mandatory Reporter Training
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3c74869 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Dec. 16, 2024

 
 

DCYF Updates Mandatory Reporter Training

*Olympia, WA –* The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) has updated its Mandatory Reporter training [ https://cpe.socialwork.uw.edu/alliance-courses/content/mandatory-reporter-elearning#group-tabs-node-course-default2 ] to highlight the distinction between families in need of support versus signs of child abuse and neglect, and the importance of connecting those families to resources.

“When poverty or other family circumstances are mistaken for neglect, this creates disparities in reporting, impacting some individuals or groups more than others,” said DCYF Assistant Secretary of Partnership, Prevention, and Services Vickie Ybarra. “In Washington, Native American/Alaska Native families, and some communities of color are disproportionality impacted by poverty and the child welfare system.”

*The updated training includes:*


  * Distinctions between poverty and neglect and available resources that mandatory reporters can use to help families 
  * Modules that address bias with the opportunity to learn how biases contribute to systemic racism 
  * Videos highlighting the impact of child abuse and neglect reports 
  * Knowledge checks that allow mandatory reporters to practice complex, real-life scenarios in a no-risk environment 

“We hope this training increases the number of mandatory reporters that connect children and families with valuable community resources — preventing intake calls for issues that could be better addressed by ongoing community supports,” said DCYF Assistant Secretary of Child Welfare Natalie Green.

For the past two years, DCYF staff from across the agency collaborated with medical, public education, court, and law enforcement professionals, Tribal partners, parent representatives, lived experts, The Alliance for Professional Development, Training, and Caregiver Excellence, as well as many others to shape this eLearning and inform changes made to DCYF web content and publications that focus on recognizing and reporting child abuse and neglect.

In 2023, more than 129,000 calls were made to the DCYF Intake Line, nearly 74% of the calls were made by mandatory reporters. Of the total calls, more than 85,000 were screened out, meaning they did not meet the legal threshold needed for child welfare intervention. According to the new training, each of these screened out calls could be an opportunity to strengthen a family – connecting them with resources to meet basic needs and increase protective factors.

To learn more, visit the updated reporting abuse and neglect [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/safety/report-abuse ] and mandatory reporting [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/safety/mandated-reporter ] webpages or begin the eLearning [ https://cpe.socialwork.uw.edu/alliance-courses/content/mandatory-reporter-elearning#group-tabs-node-course-default2 ] today.

###

communications.subject: News Release: Green Hill Taking Additional Steps to Address Growing Safety Concerns Around Contraband and New Type of Synthetic Drug
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3c80ca6 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Dec. 18, 2024

 
 

*Green Hill Taking Additional Steps to Address Growing Safety Concerns Around Contraband and New Type of Synthetic Drug*

*Olympia, WA –* Green Hill School has implemented new restrictions and increased the volume of searches at the juvenile rehabilitation facility as part of a concentrated effort to address a growing concern around contraband that is being exacerbated due to the ongoing overcrowding crisis.

In the last two weeks, Green Hill staff responded to 8 medical emergencies involving contraband.

“More young people on campus means we can’t safely monitor everyone, leading to an increased risk of contraband,” said DCYF Secretary Ross Hunter. “We are very concerned about this and feel that something tragic could happen if we don’t address the overcrowding and take aggressive steps to reduce the introduction of contraband.”

Green Hill’s population as of Dec. 17 is 239, nearly 60 young people above capacity. The facility hovered just above the 180 mark until it hit a spike in March 2024 when it jumped to 231. It has remained around 230 ever since.

Part of the challenge the facility is facing is that these medical incidents are not due to fentanyl, instead, Green Hill staff believe they are dealing with synthetic drugs that are harder to detect and easier to introduce on campus — a challenge that correctional facilities are facing across the nation.

These drugs are chemically made versions of illegal drugs but are slightly altered to avoid having them classified as illegal and harder to detect. As they become illegal, manufacturers alter the composition to avoid detection and prosecution.

“These drugs mimic some of the symptoms of overdoses but do not respond to Narcan,” said DCYF Assistant Secretary of Juvenile Rehabilitation Felice Upton. "These synthetics, often referred to as 'K2' or 'Spice,' are dangerous and can cause psychosis and medical complications such as cardiac and respiratory issues, which could lead to death.” 

Upton says Green Hill has implemented or is in the process of implementing the following safety measures to combat the smuggling of contraband:


  * Limiting resident access to units they are not assigned to 
  * Increasing the volume of searches 
  * Reducing the number of visitors per visit 
  * Implementing no contact during visitation 
  * Providing copies of mail to young people rather than the original piece of mail (except for legal mail) 
  * Implementing a clear bag policy for staff and contractors entering campus 
  * Limiting additional food from staff and contractors coming onto campus 
  * Identifying additional treatment options for young people struggling with substance use 

“I want to express my appreciation to all the staff at Green Hill, but specifically those who have been more frequent first responders,” said Interim Green Hill Superintendent Jennifer Redman. “This is not the work they show up to do but it is the harsh reality of our situation.”

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF is Accepting Nominations for Foster Youth to Throw the First Pitch at Mariners Game (English | Español)
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3cf8e72 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Feb. 7, 2025

 
 

DCYF is Accepting Nominations for Foster Youth to Throw the First Pitch at Mariners Game

*Olympia, WA – *Do you know a child or youth in foster care who loves baseball? The Washington State Department, of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) is now accepting nominations for one special child or youth in foster care to throw the ceremonial first pitch at a Mariners game.

Every year, DCYF partners with the Seattle Mariners to celebrate kinship, foster, and adoptive families during their annual event, We Are Family Day [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2025/02/04/file_attachments/3154190/We%20Are%20Family%20Day%20Flyer%202025.pdf ], held at T-Mobile Park.

*To be eligible, the child or youth must:*


  * Have experienced foster care during the past 12 months in Washington state. 
  * Be at least 10 years old. 
  * Be prepared and comfortable standing in front of a large crowd of people and throwing a baseball to home plate. 

*Nominations must include:*


  * The young person’s first name and age. 
  * Information about why you think they and their caregiver are good candidates for this opportunity. 
  * Caregiver name(s) and contact information. 

This year, in an effort to highlight the critical and supportive relationships between young people in care and their caregivers, one of the caregivers of the selected child or youth will serve as catcher.

To nominate a youth and their caregiver, email holly.luna@dcyf.wa.gov with the required information *by Friday, Feb. 28, 2025.* The selected nominee and their caregiver will be asked to complete a photo release with appropriate signatures.

We Are Family Day is a chance to watch a Seattle Mariners baseball game while supporting and celebrating the foster, kinship, and adoptive families in our communities. If you'd like to attend, get your tickets at https://www.mlb.com/mariners/tickets/specials/we-are-family.

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Communications Administrator|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

El DCYF está recibiendo postulaciones para que los jóvenes en el sistema de acogida hagan el primer lanzamiento en el partido de los Mariners

*Olympia, WA – *¿Conoce a un niño o joven que se encuentre en cuidado de acogida y ame el béisbol? El Washington State Department, of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) está recibiendo postulaciones para que un niño o joven especial en cuidado de acogida haga el primer lanzamiento ceremonial en un partido de los Mariners.

Cada año, el DCYF se asocia con los Seattle Mariners para celebrar a las familias por parentesco, de acogida y adoptivas durante su evento anual, We Are Family Day, que se lleva a cabo en el T-Mobile Park.

*Para ser elegible, el niño o joven debe cumplir con los siguientes requisitos:*


  * Haber estado en cuidado de acogida durante los últimos 12 meses en el estado de Washington. 
  * Tener al menos 10 años de edad. 
  * Estar preparado y sentirse cómodo lanzando una pelota de béisbol a la base frente a una gran multitud de personas. 

*Las postulaciones deben incluir la siguiente información:*


  * Nombre y edad del niño o joven. 
  * Una descripción de por qué cree que el niño o joven y su cuidador son buenos candidatos para esta oportunidad. 
  * Nombre completo e información de contacto del cuidador. 

Este año, con el objetivo de hacer énfasis en la importancia de la relación y el apoyo entre los menores en cuidado de acogida y sus cuidadores, uno de los cuidadores del niño o joven seleccionado será el cácher.

Para postular a un joven y su cuidador, envíe un correo electrónico a holly.luna@dcyf.wa.gov con la información solicitada a más tardar el viernes *28 de febrero de 2025.* El postulante seleccionado y su cuidador deberán completar y firmar un formulario para autorizar la publicación de fotografías.

El We Are Family Day es una oportunidad para ver un partido de béisbol de los Seattle Mariners con el fin de apoyar y celebrar a las familias por parentesco, de acogida y adoptivas de nuestras comunidades. Si le gustaría asistir al evento, puede obtener las entradas en https://www.mlb.com/mariners/tickets/specials/we-are-family.

###

*Contacto:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Administradora de Comunicaciones|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: Washington Caregivers Recognized During Parent Recognition Month (English | Español)
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3d425f6 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Feb. 25, 2025

 
 

*Washington Caregivers Recognized During Parent Recognition Month*

*Olympia, WA – *A mother who fiercely advocates for her son with autism, a father who overcame his addiction to reunify with his children, and foster parents who provided stability to children are among the 28 caregivers who will be recognized this month as Unsung Heroes. 

The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) will honor 28 Unsung Hero Award recipients - one for each day of the month – as part of Parent Recognition month. For the past 14 years, DCYF has awarded individuals who exemplify one or more of the five protective factors known to help reduce child abuse and neglect.

“These are the people who go above and beyond for the children and youth of Washington. Their commitment, hard work, and love may go unnoticed to some, but we see them and recognize the selfless support they provide to ensure children are safe and thriving,” said DCYF Secretary Tana Senn.

The five protective factors include:


  * *Parental Resilience –* the ability to bounce back from stress. 
  * *Social Connections – *access to a strong support system to help weather challenges during hard times. 
  * *Knowledge of Parenting and Child Development –* an understanding of a child’s developmental needs and the parenting strategies to support a child’s physical, cognitive, language, social, and emotional development. 
  * *Social and Emotional Competence of Children –* empathy and support toward a child’s social and emotional wellbeing. 
  * *Concrete Supports in Times of Need* –awareness of where to access essential items and resources to minimize and avoid crisis. 

Every year, DCYF partners with Seattle’s Child Magazine, which features one honoree each day in February on their website [ https://www.seattleschild.com ]. The recipients will also be recognized during a ceremony in Lacey, WA on Feb. 27.

To learn more about the protective factors, visit DCYF’s Strengthening Families Washington [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/child-development-supports/sfwa ] webpage.

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of Communications|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

Reconocen a cuidadores de Washington durante el Mes del Reconocimiento de los Padres

*Olympia, WA *– Una madre que promueve intensamente a su hijo con autismo, un padre que superó su adicción para reintegrarse con sus hijos y padres de cuidado de crianza que brindaron estabilidad a menores están entre los 28 cuidadores que serán reconocidos este mes como "Unsung Heroes" (Heroes que no son reconocidos).

El Departamento de Niños, Jóvenes y Familias del Estado de Washington (DCYF, por sus siglas en inglés) rendirá homenaje a 28 ganadores del Premio "Unsung Heroes," uno por cada día del mes, como parte del Mes del Reconocimiento de los Padres. Durante los últimos 14 años, el DCYF ha premiado a personas que ejemplifican uno o más de los cinco factores de protección que sabemos que ayudan a reducir el maltrato y abandono infantil.

"Estas son personas que van más allá del cumplimiento del deber por los niños y jóvenes de Washington. Su compromiso, arduo trabajo y amor podrían pasar desapercibidos para algunos, pero nosotros los vemos y reconocemos el apoyo generoso que brindan para garantizar la seguridad y el desarrollo de los niños", dijo la secretaria del DCYF, Tara Senn.

Los cinco factores de protección son:


  * *Resiliencia parental. *La capacidad de recuperarse del estrés. 
  * *Condiciones sociales. *El acceso a un sólido sistema de apoyo para ayudar a superar los desafíos en épocas difíciles. 
  * *Conocimientos sobre la crianza y el desarrollo infantil.* Comprensión de las necesidades del desarrollo infantil y las estrategias de crianza para ayudar al desarrollo físico, cognitivo, lingüístico, social y emocional de un niño. 
  * *Competencia social y emocional de los niños. *Empatía y apoyo para ayudar al bienestar socioemocional del niño. 
  * *Apoyos concretos en momentos de necesidad.* Conocimiento de dónde obtener acceso a artículos y recursos esenciales para minimizar y evitar crisis. 

Cada año, el DCYF se asocia con Seattle's Child Magazine, que destaca a uno de galardonados en su sitio web [ https://www.seattleschild.com ] en cada día de febrero. Los ganadores también recibirán un reconocimiento en una ceremonia celebrada el 27 de febrero en Lacey, WA.

Para ver más información sobre los factores de protección, visite la página web Fortalecer a las familias de Washington [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/child-development-supports/sfwa ] del DCYF.

###

Nancy Gutierrez, Directora de Comunicación | 360-742-6418 | Nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: Pierce County Youth to Throw Ceremonial First Pitch During April 27 Mariners Game (English | Español)
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3dad5fd ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

April 8, 2025

 
 

Pierce County Youth to Throw Ceremonial First Pitch During April 27 Mariners Game  

*Olympia, WA – *A 15-year-old from Pierce County will be throwing the ceremonial first pitch during the April 27 Seattle Mariners game as part of We Are Family Day, an event celebrating the dedication and heart of foster parents and kinship caregivers.

The Washington State Department of Children, Youth and Families (DCYF) selected Tyson as this year’s First Pitch Kid, an honor given to a child or youth in out-of-home care nominated by their DCYF caseworker or care team.

Tyson is a multi-sport athlete, choir member, and avid Seattle Mariners fan who is excited to be throwing the ceremonial first pitch on behalf of foster children, youth, and their caregivers.

Tyson’s friends and family describe him as a very positive, friendly teenager that enjoys being involved in his community, interacting with his peers, and playing sports. He is a proud member of his high school choir, helps coach soccer at this former middle school, and is on the honor roll.

For the past 17 years, DCYF has partnered with the Seattle Mariners as part of Foster Care Month, which is celebrated in May. Foster Care Month is an opportunity to raise awareness about the needs of children and youth in foster care and to celebrate the foster parents and kinship caregivers who support them.

In Washington state, there are more than 4,800 licensed foster homes (this includes community foster and kinship) and more than 4,700 children and youth in out-of-home care.

“Great outcomes for foster youth don’t happen overnight,” said DCYF Secretary Tana Senn. “It takes long-term engagement by many caring adults in their lives, including foster parents and kinship caregivers. While the number of children and youth in out-of-home care has decreased over the years, foster parents continue to play a critical role in supporting families in crisis, keeping kids in their community, and providing a safe place to call home. We so appreciate all our kinship caregivers and foster parents!”

We Are Family Day Tickets are available for purchase. [ https://www.mlb.com/mariners/tickets/specials/we-are-family ]

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of Communications|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

Joven del condado de Pierce hará el primer lanzamiento ceremonial durante el juego de los Mariners del 27 de abril

*Olympia, WA *– Un joven de 15 años del condado de Pierce estará a cargo del primer lanzamiento ceremonial en el juego de los Seattle Mariners del 27 de abril como parte del día “Somos familia”, un evento que celebra la dedicación y el corazón de los padres de acogida y los cuidadores familiares.

El Washington State Department of Children, Youth and Families (DCYF) eligió a Tyson para realizar el primer lanzamiento de este año, un honor que se le otorga a un niño o joven en situación de acogida, elegido por el asistente social o el equipo de asistencia del DCYF.

Tyson es un atleta de múltiples deportes, miembro del coro y gran fanático de los Seattle Mariners. Está muy emocionado por realizar el lanzamiento ceremonial de la primera bola en representación de todos los niños, jóvenes y cuidadores de acogida.

Los amigos y la familia de Tyson lo describen como un adolescente muy positivo y amistoso, que disfruta de desenvolverse en su comunidad, interactuar con sus pares y hacer deportes. Se enorgullece de formar parte del coro de su escuela secundaria, colabora con el instructor de fútbol de su antigua escuela primaria y, además, pertenece al cuadro de honor.

Durante los últimos 17 años, el DCYF se ha asociado con los Seattle Mariners como parte del Mes del Cuidado de Acogida Familiar, que se celebra en mayo. El Mes del Cuidado de Acogida Familiar es una oportunidad para concientizar sobre las necesidades de los niños y jóvenes en cuidado de acogida y celebrar a los padres de acogida y a los cuidadores familiares que los apoyan.

En el estado de Washington, hay más de 4800 hogares de acogida autorizados (esto incluye acogida comunitaria y familiar) y más de 4700 niños y jóvenes en acogida fuera del hogar familiar.

“Los buenos resultados para los jóvenes de acogida no se consiguen de la noche a la mañana”, dice Tana Senn, secretaria del DCYF. “Se necesita un compromiso a largo plazo de muchos adultos responsables, incluidos padres de acogida y cuidadores familiares. Mientras el número de niños y jóvenes en acogida fuera del hogar familiar ha disminuido a lo largo de los años, los padres de acogida siguen desempeñando un papel fundamental al apoyar a familias en crisis, mantener a los niños en su comunidad y brindarles un hogar seguro. ¡Apreciamos mucho a todos nuestros cuidadores familiares y padres de acogida!”

Las entradas para el día “Somos familia” ya están disponibles para la venta. [ https://www.mlb.com/mariners/tickets/specials/we-are-family ]

###

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF to Celebrate Fathers Who Overcome Obstacles to Reunite with their Children (English | Español)
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3dd797d ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

April 24, 2025

 
 

DCYF to Celebrate Fathers Who Overcome Obstacles to Reunite with their Children

*Olympia, WA – *Do you know a father who has overcome personal obstacles and engaged in services to safely reunite with their child? The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) is now accepting nominations for their annual Engaging Fathers Award, which celebrates the importance of a positive and healthy relationship between fathers and their children.

The Engaging Father Award recognizes fathers who have had a successful outcome with their child welfare case. DCYF is planning a prize for the recipient who will be celebrated during a virtual award ceremony the Thursday before Father’s Day.

*To be eligible, the father:*


  * Must have a dependency case within the past 12 months 
  * Must live in Washington State 

*Nominations need to include the following:*


  * The father’s name 
  * Why they should be recognized 
  * Examples of the father demonstrating positive behavioral changes (ex: participating in the process) 

*Nominations must be sent to dcyf.recognition@dcyf.wa.gov by Thursday, May 1, 2025.*

Prior recipients [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/news/pierce-county-father-honored-engaging-father-award ] of the award were recognized for seeking treatment, finding sobriety, and making significant life changes to regain custody of their children.

The Engaging Father award is part of DCYF's Engaging Fathers project, an engagement effort for fathers with children in the child welfare system. Child welfare agencies have historically not engaged enough with fathers. As a result, fathers are less willing to engage. However, research shows [ https://www.fatherhood.gov/research-and-resources/meta-analysis-relationship-between-father-involvement-and-student-academic ] that a father's role in a child's life impacts their growth and shapes their personality and development.

“Research shows that when fathers are involved in their children’s lives, children perform better in school, exhibit healthier behavior, and show positive growth and development,” said Secretary Tana Senn. “This award provides an opportunity to highlight fathers who may have been in crises or made mistakes but make the choice to engage in services and turn their lives around for themselves and their children. These inspiring stories demonstrate what can be achieved when DCYF partners with dads.”

Learn more about the program by visiting the Engaging Fathers webpage. [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/engaging-fathers ]

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of Communications|360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

El DCYF celebrará a los padres que superan obstáculos para reunirse con sus hijos

*Olympia, WA *– ¿Conoce a un padre que haya superado obstáculos personales y participado en servicios para reunirse de forma segura con su hijo? El Departamento de Niños, Jóvenes y Familias (Department of Children, Youth, and Families, DCYF) está recibiendo nominaciones para su premio anual Engaging Fathers (Padres Comprometidos), que celebra la importancia de una relación positiva y saludable entre padres e hijos.

El premio Engaging Father tiene el objetivo de reconocer a los padres que obtuvieron un resultado satisfactorio en su caso de bienestar infantil. DCYF está organizando un reconocimiento para el ganador que se llevará a cabo en una ceremonia virtual el jueves antes del Día del Padre

*Para participar, el padre debe cumplir con los siguientes requisitos:*


  * Debe tener un caso de tutela dentro de los últimos 12 meses. 
  * Debe vivir en el estado de Washington. 

*Las nominaciones deben incluir lo siguiente:*


  * el nombre del padre 
  * el motivo por el que merece reconocimiento 
  * ejemplos del padre que demuestren cambios de comportamiento positivos (por ejemplo, su participación en el proceso) 

*Las nominaciones deben enviarse adcyf.recognition@dcyf.wa.gov antes del jueves 1 de mayo de 2025.*

Los ganadores anteriores del premio fueron reconocidos por buscar tratamiento, lograr la sobriedad y realizar cambios de vida importantes para recuperar la custodia de sus hijos.

El premio Engaging Father es parte del proyecto Engaging Fathers del DCYF, una iniciativa de participación de los padres con hijos en el sistema de bienestar infantil. En el pasado, las agencias de bienestar infantil no se habían involucrado lo suficiente con los padres. Como resultado, los padres están menos dispuestos a involucrarse. Sin embargo,las investigaciones muestran [ https://www.fatherhood.gov/research-and-resources/meta-analysis-relationship-between-father-involvement-and-student-academic ] que el papel del padre en la vida de un niño afecta su crecimiento y da forma a su personalidad y desarrollo.

"Los estudios demuestran que cuando los padres están involucrados en la vida de sus hijos, los hijos logran una mejor participación académica, un comportamiento más saludable y muestran un crecimiento y desarrollo favorable" declarό La Secretaria Tana Senn. "Este reconocimiento nos da la oportunidad de destacar a los padres que pasaron por una crisis o que tuvieron errores, y quienes tomaron la decisión de participar en servicios para cambiar sus vidas y la de sus hijos. Estas historias inspiradoras nos demuestran los logros que se pueden hacer cuando los padres y DCYF colaboran." 

Obtenga más información sobre el programa consultando la página web de Engaging Fathers. [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/engaging-fathers ]

###

*Contacto:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Directora de Comunicación | 360-742-6418 | Nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: Harbor Heights Superintendent Hopes to Inspire Young People at New JR Facility
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3e259d4 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

May 28, 2025

 
 

Harbor Heights Superintendent Hopes to Inspire Young People at New JR Facility

*Olympia, WA — *Growing up in a rough neighborhood, without a father, and losing a best friend to gun violence, Harbor Heights Superintendent Kendrick ‘Ken’ Rochelle hopes to inspire young people by showing them that regardless of where they come from, there is always hope.

“Some of the stories they have, I have. The difference is the seat I sit in now, which required me to take the right path and work hard,” said Rochelle, who will lead Washington’s newest juvenile rehabilitation facility.

Rochelle brings 26 years of juvenile rehabilitation experience to Harbor Heights, starting his career as an entry level correctional officer and moving up the ranks before becoming a superintendent during his tenures in Ohio, Louisiana, Arkansas, and Texas.

He also brings years of experience mentoring young people and pushing them to greatness as a high school football coach.

“It’s like I have played every position, so I can empathize with my staff because I’ve been in similar shoes,” says Rochelle. “Much like football, it takes a team to succeed.”

Rochelle says he was drawn to the educational, trade, and work opportunities young people have at DCYF facilities. He also looks forward to building a culture that revolves around safety and allows young people to take on leadership skills, build accountability, and create future paths for success.

“You have to show young people that violence is not the answer,” said Rochelle. “We want to change their thinking, so they are productive and leave our facilities having built a different future with reasons not to come back.”

Rochelle has met dozens of young people at Green Hill over the last few weeks as part of recruitment efforts for Harbor Heights’ first cohort. For those who have met Rochelle, it comes as no surprise that after meeting him, many young people have shown an interest in applying to Harbor Heights.

“You have to chip away the walls to build relationships, be genuine, and consistently show up for these young people,” said Rochelle. “It’s a balance. You have to be firm, fair, caring, and consistent.”

Though Harbor Heights will not resolve Green Hill’s overcrowding challenges, the opening of the facility provides some immediate relief and, more importantly, harbors hope. The initial cohort will include eight young people and increase to 46 over time.

“Harbor Heights will give young people and staff some breathing room at Green Hill to work on the treatment they need,” said Rochelle. “I believe that change can happen. It can’t rain forever.”

Harbor Heights is set to open early June. The young men at the facility will receive leadership and therapy skills they can use when they transition to another facility or return to the community. Programming will also include automative training and peer counseling certification.

“Harbor Heights is going to look different than our other JR facilities, but this is what brought me to Washington,” said Rochelle. “The tools we are giving these young men and the space to put it to practice is what rehabilitation is all about.”

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of Communications | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: New Juvenile Rehabilitation Facility Opens in WA State
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3e4730e ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

June 13, 2025

 
 

New Juvenile Rehabilitation Facility Opens in WA State   
Harbor Heights common space image 

*Olympia, WA —*The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) welcomed the first cohort of young men to Harbor Heights, the state’s newest juvenile rehabilitation facility. 

Harbor Heights officially opened this week, welcoming eight young men from Green Hill School who will participate in curated programming that provides behavior therapy, peer counseling, and workforce development.  

The first cohort is made up of young men who applied to Harbor Heights and were selected after meeting certain criteria and participating in an interview. 

Harbor Heights Superintendent Ken Rochelle  [ https://content.govdelivery.com/bulletins/gd/WADEL-3e259d4?wgt_ref=WADEL_WIDGET_277 ]personally met with the young men while they were at Green Hill to discuss the programming that he hopes will allow young people to take on leadership skills, build accountability, and create future paths for success.   

“Something that brought me to Washington was the programming” said Superintendent Rochelle during a media preview event at Harbor Heights [ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9s-c7YGVoPQ ]. “I see opportunities here that give the young men a chance to not recommit.” 

One of the unique programs available at Harbor Heights is a virtual reality automotive repair training through Vehicles for Change. The young men will also receive mentorship and can earn a peer counselor certification through a partnership with Northwest Credible Messengers. 

Staff at the facility received Cognitive Behavior Therapy (CBT) training through the Roca Impact institute (RII). Unlike traditional CBT, RII’s Rewire CBT is not limited to a clinical setting, which means trained frontline staff can use this trauma-informed approach on young people throughout the facility. This skills-based intervention helps young people heal from trauma and learn skills to regulate emotions.  

“Harbor Heights is an investment in our young men, providing new opportunities, new hope and the space to grow,” said DCYF Secretary Tana Senn.  

Though the facility is located on the grounds of the Stafford Creek Correctional Center in Aberdeen, Harbor Heights will be run by DCYF with its own visitation area, medical services, and recreation field. The young men will not interact with those in the custody of the Department of Corrections. 

The opening of the facility comes after DCYF explored dozens of sites across the state to address overcrowding challenges. The Aberdeen facility was selected because it allowed DCYF to begin operations in a matter of months, was cost effective compared to other options that required extensive renovations, and the site would be accessible to families for visits. 

“Harbor Heights is critical to help relieve some of the pressure at Green Hill School so both young people and staff have increased safety and more options, “explained Senn. “While this is an important start, it isn’t going to solve the overcrowding issue.” 

To learn more, visit the Harbor Heights webpage [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/juvenile-rehabilitation/harbor-heights ]. 

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of Communications | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

communications.subject: News Release: WA State Makes it Easier for Relatives to Become Licensed Caregivers
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3e7ba28 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

July 2, 2025

 
 

WA State Makes it Easier for Relatives to Become Licensed Caregivers

*Olympia, WA — *The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) launched new licensing standards that make it easier for relatives to become licensed caregivers, which provides additional supports to care for children and youth in foster care.

The new Kinship Licensing Standards — launched July 1 — include 50% less forms for applicants to fill out. The new standards also established a kinship home study with less questions than the current community foster care licensing process.

Prior to these standards, relatives were still able to get licensed, but many of the questions and forms were repetitive and did not apply to a kin-specific situation. This new process was tailored for relatives and removed barriers that often-discouraged them from attaining a license while maintaining the same level of safety for the children and youth involved.

“Kinship caregivers often face unique challenges that could be addressed if they got licensed,” said DCYF’s Assistant Secretary of Licensing Division Ruben Reeves. “By offering a process tailored for relatives, we can get more relatives licensed and give them access to consistent resources, training, and support so children in kinship care have the best possible chance at healthy development and long-term stability.”

DCYF’s main goals for the new standards were to:


  * Create a simplified, streamlined process to license kinship caregivers 
  * Promote equity by taking steps to address systemic imbalances 
  * Make sure that all impacted groups and Tribal partners were part of creating new standards 

Licensing is critical as it opens up opportunities for financial reimbursement, respite, and other supports that help meet the needs of the children and youth being served as well as the caregivers themselves.

Washington state is one of the leading states in the kinship space, adopting a kin-first culture early on to address the disparities faced by relatives who fostered. As of May 2025, nearly 59% of children in out-of-home care in Washington state are placed with kin, and 68.2% of those children are with a fully licensed kin caregiver, a number that the agency is hoping will grow as the new process is adopted.

“We know that children and youth in foster care do better when placed with relatives,” said DCYF Secretary Tana Senn. “They do better emotionally and developmentally and stay more connected to their communities and culture. These new standards are an effort to be more customer service oriented, to ensure stable placements and to show our support for those doing the incredibly important work of caring for our children and youth.”

View the new Kinship Licensing Rules [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/foster-parenting/kinship-caregivers/rules ] and Kinship Caregiver Resources [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/foster-parenting/kinship-caregivers/resources/payments ].

###

communications.subject: News Release: WA Offering Coaching to Prospective Child Care Providers
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3ee6206 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Aug. 18, 2025

 
 

WA Offering Coaching to Prospective Child Care Providers

*Olympia, WA – *As part of statewide efforts to make it easier for licensed child care providers to open their doors, the Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) is now offering coaching services to help individuals during the application process. 

This week, DCYF launched the Pre-Licensing Services Team, which includes licensors statewide, to help individuals wanting to become licensed child care providers. 

“By providing support before an application is ever submitted, we can help prospective providers be better prepared and successful in opening their doors to families,” said Assistant Secretary of Licensing Ruben Reeves.  

The Pre-Licensing Team will help applicants: 


  * Understand the Washington Administrative Code  

  * Navigate the challenges of opening a program  

  * Build knowledge of operating child care programs in different regions of the state   

  * Understand the tools and resources specific to the different types of licenses  

Once the Pre-Licensing Services team receive an inquiry from individuals seeking to become licensed, the team will coach them through the licensing application process, and schedule in-person onboarding sessions. 

“We are always looking for individuals who can provide quality child care to Washington families,” said Reeves. “This is just another way for us to let them know, we are here to help.” 

If you are interested in becoming a licensed child care provider, visit the Licensing Process webpage: https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/early-learning-providers/licensed-provider/licensing-process [ https://mcas-proxyweb.mcas-gov.ms/certificate-checker?login=false&originalUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fdcyf.wa.gov.mcas-gov.ms%2Fservices%2Fearly-learning-providers%2Flicensed-provider%2Flicensing-process%3FMcasTsid%3D15600&McasCSRF=2f2b183dcaa04aca5b8bbbb0f1ea130342b6b0ecc81f810c7b71c54ae4adf6db ] or email dcyf.welcome@dcyf.wa.gov.

communications.subject: News Release: Young People at Echo Glen Help Train Rescue Dogs
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3efe502 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

August 28, 2025

 
 

Young People at Echo Glen Help Train Rescue Dogs

Dog Training photo  

*Olympia, WA —*For the last 25 years, young people at Echo Glen have found that both old dogs and puppies can learn new tricks.  

Through the Canine Connection Program at Echo Glen, young people are matched with dogs in need of a home.  The young people train and prepare the dogs for adoption and in return the young people are taught valuable skills around dealing with emotions.   

This year, the program is celebrating 25 years and the more than 1,600  dogs that have since been rescued and adopted, including 18 service dogs. 

“I learned a lot of patience while working with dogs. They teach you patience, strength, and how to communicate,” said one young person. “I was angry I was here but one day I was like ‘if I fight then I can’t walk my dog and then who will?” 

Many of the young people feel a sense of responsibility toward their assigned dog. During their shift at the kennels, they clean, teach tricks, and even sing to the dogs. At the same time, young people mirror Dialectal Behavioral Therapy, which focuses on managing emotions and encouraging positive behaviors. 

“Dogs can be misunderstood like us, they can be shy at first and might even bark, but after a while we get to see their personalities when they open up,” said one participant.  

Canine Connections Program Coordinator Christina Reeves says young people who enter the kennels sometimes come in with a tough exterior but once they spend time with the dogs they end up a different person. They end up advocating for their dog, take responsibility, and pride in the work they do with their dog.  

“I love cleaning and knowing at the end of the day they have special toys and a bed and clean water” said one young person. “I can go back to the cottage knowing they are in a safe space.”  

Although the dogs are adopted, many of the young people are grateful for the time with the dogs and happy that they are out of the kennels in their forever homes.  

 “Just walking in makes my day better and I feel like I can handle challenges,” said one young person. “Being in this environment and realizing there is a community that doesn’t see me for a crime. They just see me as someone who loves dogs.” 

If you are interest in adopting one of the dogs, visit https://www.petfinder.com/search/pets-for-adoption/?shelter_id%5B0%5D=WA23&sort%5B0%5D=recently_added

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Hires Assistant Secretary of JR
communications.communication:
The agency also welcomes Green Hill School’s new Superintendent.  

 

Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3f13bc4 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Sept. 5, 2025

 
 

*DCYF Hires Assistant Secretary of JR*

"The agency also welcomes Green Hill School’s new Superintendent."

*Olympia, WA — *With over 30 years of experience in the juvenile justice space, Jennifer Redman has been tapped as the new Assistant Secretary of Juvenile Rehabilitation (JR) for the Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) starting Sept. 16.

Redman, who most recently served as the Director of Operations, Safety, and Security while simultaneously filling in as the interim superintendent of Green Hill School, stepped up during a critical time in JR and successfully improved conditions at Green Hill over the last few months.

Under Redman’s leadership, JR saw:


  * Significant reduction in the number of aggression incidents at Green Hill 
  * Staffing and safety improvements 
  * Expansion of youth programming and a reduction in idle time 
  * Safe and successful transition out of Incident Command status at Green Hill 
  * Successful planning and transition of the first cohort of young people to the new Harbor Heights facility 

Her deep understanding of the challenges and needs of JR was developed after serving various roles over the past three decades, moving up the ranks since she began her career as a security officer at Green Hill.

“When you’ve been in the shoes of entry-level staff, as well as those more seasoned, you can understand the needs and challenges staff face every day to deliver treatment to young people,” said Secretary Tana Senn. “I’m confident that with Jennifer Redman at the reins, she will look at the full continuum of JR services and offerings to improve conditions and outcomes for the young people we serve, while addressing staff safety needs, as well.”

Prior to her time at Green Hill, Redman served as associate superintendent of Maple Lane, a then-JR facility with a focus on behavioral health and treatment that provides wraparound supports to young people with high mental health needs. Her first-hand knowledge of dealing with high-risk youth and helping them reintegrate with their families and communities provides a framework for how she approaches juvenile rehabilitation.

“Safety is my number one priority. We need to foster environments that are healthy and equip staff with the tools they need to provide services so young people can successfully transition to be productive members in the community,” said Redman.

DCYF will also welcome Green Hill’s newest superintendent Harold Wright, Jr., who will lead the Chehalis facility starting Sept. 16. Wright most recently served as the Deputy Director for the Office of Health Professions at the Department of Health. He brings over two decades of experience working with at-risk youth within the behavioral health and diversion space. Before joining state government, Harold worked at Pioneer Human Services where he worked closely on juvenile reentry programming. During this time, he worked with young people with high needs, helping them successfully transition out of juvenile rehabilitation and foster care.

“Whether it’s earning a high school diploma, gaining skills to manage difficult situations, or learning a trade, we want young people to leave JR better than when they arrived,” said Redman. “Their success will have a direct impact on community safety.”

Jennifer Redman Headshot [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2025/09/05/file_attachments/3379800/RedmanHeadshot.jpg ]

Harold Wright Jr. Headshot [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2025/09/05/file_attachments/3379791/WrightJrHeadshot.jpeg ]

communications.subject: News Release: Washington Celebrates Adoption of Foster Youth, Children (Espanol)
communications.communication:
View a full list of Adoption Day events across the state.  

 

Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3fade98 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Nov. 17, 2025

 
 

Washington Celebrates Adoption of Foster Youth, Children
family of three smiling with judge and mariners mascot celebrating their adoption finalization

*Olympia, WA — *Communities across Washington are celebrating the adoption of children and youth out of foster care during special ceremonies this month, as they join the nation in celebrating Adoption Day.

Since Jan. 1, 2025, more than 400 children and youth have been adopted in Washington state. This month, dozens of families will finalize their adoption in courthouses, from Seattle to Spokane.

“A nurturing family makes such a difference in the life of a child,” said Washington Supreme Court Justice and co-chair of the Supreme Court Commission on Children in Foster Care Barbara A. Madsen. “We cannot possibly measure it, but we know the joy these adoptions bring to the lives of children and their adoptive parents, as well as the immense value to our communities.”

For more than two decades, the Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) and the Administrative Office of the Courts (AOC) have hosted National Adoption Day receptions to raise awareness around the continued need for adoptive families.

Currently, more than 700 children in the Washington foster care system are ready to be adopted.

“An adoption is a life changing event for everyone involved but for that child it means stability, safety, and belonging,” said DCYF Secretary Tana Senn, who also serves as co-chair of the Supreme Court Commission on Children in Foster Care. “They say it ‘takes a village’ and that concept comes to life when we see families in the community open their homes and hearts to a child or youth, helping them exit foster care and find permanency.”

Gov. Bob Ferguson proclaimed [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2025/11/10/file_attachments/3456668/Adoption%20Day%20Proclamation%20November%202025.pdf ] Nov. 21 as Adoption Day in Washington to acknowledge the critical and significant commitment individuals make when they adopt. Secretary Senn plans to celebrate with families at the Thurston County Family & Juvenile Court Adoption Day ceremony on Nov. 20.

To find out when your county is celebrating, view the full event list [ https://www.courts.wa.gov/newsinfo/adoptionDay/ ].

Learn about the adoption process [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption/steps-to-adoption ] on DCYF's website. 

You can also find information about the Adoption Support Program [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption-support-program ], which removes barriers for families adopting special needs children by providing financial, medical, and counseling support to families who adopt from Washington state.

###

Adoption Day B-Roll Footage [ http://www.dcyf.wa.gov/sites/default/files/media/adoption-media-b-roll.zip ]

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of External Communications, DCYF | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

Lorrie Thompson, Senior Communications Officer, AOC | 360-705-5347 |lorrie.Thompson@courts.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

Washington celebra la adopción de niños y jóvenes del sistema de acogimiento familiar

*Olympia, WA — *En noviembre, las comunidades de todo el estado de Washington celebrarán la adopción de niños y jóvenes que se encuentran en el sistema de acogimiento familiar durante ceremonias especiales, sumándose así a la celebración nacional del Día de la Adopción.

Desde el 1 de enero de 2025, más de 400 niños y jóvenes han sido adoptados en el estado de Washington. Este mes, decenas de familias finalizarán su proceso de adopción en los juzgados, desde Seattle hasta Spokane.

“Una familia afectuosa marca una gran diferencia en la vida de un niño”, expresó Barbara A. Madsen, jueza de la Corte Suprema de Washington y copresidenta de la Supreme Court Commission on Children in Foster Care. “No podemos medirlo, pero sabemos la alegría que estas adopciones traen a la vida de los niños y sus padres adoptivos, así como el inmenso valor que aportan a nuestras comunidades.”

Durante más de dos décadas, el Departamento de Niños, Jóvenes y Familias (Department of Children, Youth, and Families, DCYF) y la Oficina Administrativa de los Tribunales (Administrative Office of the Courts, AOC) han organizado recepciones en conmemoración del Día Nacional de la Adopción para generar conciencia sobre la constante necesidad de familias adoptivas.

Actualmente, más de 700 niños en el sistema de acogimiento familiar de Washington están listos para ser adoptados.

“La adopción es un acontecimiento que cambia la vida de todos los involucrados, pero para ese niño significa estabilidad, seguridad y pertenencia”, indicó la secretaria del DCYF, Tana Senn, quien también funge como copresidenta de la Supreme Court Commission on Children in Foster Care. “Dicen que ‘se necesita un pueblo entero’ y ese concepto cobra vida cuando vemos a familias de la comunidad abrir sus hogares y corazones a un niño o joven para ayudarles a salir del sistema de acogimiento familiar y encontrar la estabilidad permanente.”

El gobernador Bob Ferguson proclamó [ https://content.govdelivery.com/attachments/WADEL/2025/11/10/file_attachments/3456668/Adoption%20Day%20Proclamation%20November%202025.pdf ] el 21 de noviembre como el Día de la Adopción en Washington para reconocer el compromiso crucial y significativo que se asume al adoptar. La secretaria Senn planea celebrar con las familias en la ceremonia del Día de la Adopción del tribunal de familia y menores del condado de Thurston el 20 de noviembre.

Para saber cuándo se celebra en su condado, consulte la lista completa de eventos [ https://www.courts.wa.gov/newsinfo/adoptionDay/ ].

Infórmese sobre el proceso de adopción [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption/steps-to-adoption ] en el sitio web de DCYF.

También puede encontrar información sobre el programa de apoyo a la adopción Adoption Support Program [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/services/adoption-support-program ], que elimina las barreras para las familias que adoptan niños con necesidades especiales y brinda apoyo financiero, médico y de asesoramiento a las familias que adoptan en el estado de Washington.

###

Imágenes del Día de la Adopción [ http://www.dcyf.wa.gov/sites/default/files/media/adoption-media-b-roll.zip ]

communications.subject: Media Advisory: DCYF Welcomes New Chief Public Affairs Officer
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/3fe5a51 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Dec. 10, 2025

 
 

DCYF Welcomes New Chief Public Affairs Officer

Barbara Serrano headshot 

*Olympia, WA — *The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) has welcomed *Barbara Serrano* as its newest Chief Public Affairs Officer.  

"I’m thrilled to welcome Barbara—along with her expertise and leadership—to our agency," said Secretary Tana Senn. "Her insights and experience will undoubtedly broaden our reach and deepen our impact as we continue to improve long-term outcomes for the children, youth, and families we serve."

Prior to joining DCYF, Barbara served as Deputy Chief of Staff at the Department of Social and Health Services (DSHS), where she oversaw risk management and other administrative services. 

She also previously served as Senior Policy Advisor on Public Safety for Gov. Jay Inslee. In that role, Barbara worked closely with DCYF leaders to address overcrowding and safety issues at Green Hill School and served as the governor’s representative for the Partnership Council on Juvenile Justice and the DCYF Oversight Board. 

• Barbara Serrano's Bio [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/about/our-leadership/barbara-serrano ]

You can reach Barbara by emailing barbara.serrano@dcyf.wa.gov.

communications.subject: News Release: DCYF Looking to Recognize Parents, Caregivers, Guardians (English | Espanol)
communications.communication:
Having trouble viewing this email? View it as a Web page [ https://content.govdelivery.com/accounts/WADEL/bulletins/4012256 ].

 
 

DCYF Header Image  
 

Dec. 23, 2025

 
 

  * DCYF Looking to Recognize Parents, Caregivers, Guardians [ #link_1 ] 
  * El DCYF quiere reconocer a padres, madres, cuidadores y guardianes [ #link_2 ] 

DCYF Looking to Recognize Parents, Caregivers, Guardians

The agency is accepting nominations through Jan. 6 for its Unsung Hero Awards.   

  * Unsung Hero Awards B-roll [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/sites/default/files/media/yt-0904-unsung-b-roll.zip ] 

*Olympia, WA —*The Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) is now accepting nominations for its Unsung Hero Awards,  which recognizes parents, caregivers, and guardians who have shown strength, courage, and empathy. 

“We have countless ‘unsung heroes’ in our communities that deserve recognition for the extraordinary support and care they provide to the children and youth of Washington,” said Assistant Secretary for Partnerships, Prevention, and Services Dr. Vickie Ybarra. “Their selfless advocacy helps children and youth feel safe and give them the confidence to excel.” 

The Unsung Hero Awards specially recognizes parents, caregivers, and guardians who demonstrate one or more of the five protective factors, which are known to help reduce child abuse and neglect. 

The five protective factors include: 


  * *Parental Resilience* – the ability to bounce back from stress.  

  * 

*Concrete Supports in Times of Need *– awareness of where to access essential items and resources to minimize and avoid crisis. 


  * *Social and Emotional Competence of Children* – empathy and support toward a child’s social and emotional wellbeing.  

  * *Social Connections* – access to a strong support system to help weather challenges during hard times.  

  * *Knowledge of Parenting and Child Development* – an understanding of a child’s developmental needs and the parenting strategies to support a child’s physical, cognitive, language, social, and emotional development.  

Nominations [ https://links-2.govdelivery.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fdcyf.wa.gov%2Fforms%3Ffield_number_value=02-026%26title=/1/0101019b2e13ff63-70eb41a8-e726-4a28-a70d-1f41a1bfbb78-000000/jTfGnmQ22p0lQo1BIAJdsRcImz8XGyCrCuNh26HJKBI=436 ] are due Jan. 6, 2026, and can be submitted to strengtheningfamilies@dcyf.wa.gov. Twenty-eight honorees will be celebrated in February as part of Parent Recognition Month and will be featured in Seattle’s Child Magazine [ https://links-2.govdelivery.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.seattleschild.com%2F/1/0101019b2e13ff63-70eb41a8-e726-4a28-a70d-1f41a1bfbb78-000000/lDlzSrSxbUnh-VjQZVqZrhxYe2gpq9sElVtSzjB-z0A=436 ]. 

Since 2011, DCYF has honored 394 individuals in Washington for the roles they have taken with their families, school, and communities. 

To learn more about the five protective factors, visit DCYF’s Strengthening Families Washington [ https://links-2.govdelivery.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.dcyf.wa.gov%2Fservices%2Fchild-development-supports%2Fsfwa/1/0101019b2e13ff63-70eb41a8-e726-4a28-a70d-1f41a1bfbb78-000000/e7uEOuOlKGrWvE0eMxUej831DAoDSp16TPEot_fM4i4=436 ] webpage. 

###

*Contact:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Director of External Communications, DCYF | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov

________________________________________________________________________

 

El DCYF quiere reconocer a padres, madres, cuidadores y guardianes

La agencia acepta nominaciones hasta el 6 de enero para los premios Héroes anónimos (Unsung Heroes).

  * Imágenes del los premios al Héroe No Reconocido [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/sites/default/files/media/yt-0904-unsung-b-roll.zip ] 

*Olympia, WA — *El Washington State Department of Children, Youth, and Families (DCYF) ya está aceptando nominaciones para los Premios al Héroe No Reconocido (Unsung Hero Awards), que reconocerán a padres, madres, cuidadores y guardianes que han demostrado fortaleza, coraje y empatía*.*

“Hay innumerables héroes en nuestras comunidades que merecen reconocimiento por el extraordinario apoyo y cuidado que brindan a los niños y jóvenes de Washington”, dijo la Subsecretaria de Asociaciones, Prevención y Servicios (Partnerships, Prevention, and Services), Dra. Vickie Ybarra. “Su compromiso desinteresado ayuda a los niños y jóvenes a sentirse seguros y les da la confianza para sobresalir”.

Los premios reconocen especialmente a los padres, madres, cuidadores y guardianes que demuestran uno o más de los cinco factores de protección, que se sabe que ayudan a reducir el abuso y la negligencia infantil.

Los cinco factores de protección incluyen los siguientes:


  * *Resiliencia de los padres: *la capacidad de recuperarse del estrés. 
  * *Apoyos concretos en tiempos de necesidad:* ser consciente de dónde acceder a los artículos y recursos esenciales para minimizar y evitar las crisis. 
  * *Capacidad social y emocional de los niños: *empatía y apoyo hacia el bienestar social y emocional del niño. 
  * *Conexiones sociales: *el acceso a un sólido sistema de apoyo para ayudar a superar los retos en tiempos difíciles. 
  * *Conocimiento sobre crianza y el desarrollo infantil: *una comprensión de las necesidades de desarrollo de los niños y de las estrategias de crianza que apoyan el desarrollo físico, cognitivo, lingüístico, social y emocional. 

Las nominaciones [ https://dcyf.wa.gov/forms?field_number_value=02-026&title= ] deben entregarse antes del 6 de enero de 2026 y pueden enviarse a strengtheningfamilies@dcyf.wa.gov. Se celebrará a veintiocho personas distinguidas en febrero, en el marco del Mes de Reconocimiento a los Padres y Madres y sus historias se publicarán en la revista Seattle’s Child [ https://www.seattleschild.com/ ].

Desde 2011, el DCYF ha honrado a 394 personas en Washington por los papeles que han tomado con sus familias, escuelas y comunidades. Para obtener más información sobre los cinco factores de protección, visite la página web de Fortalecimiento de las familias de Washington [ https://www.dcyf.wa.gov/services/child-development-supports/sfwa ] del DCYF.

###

 

*Contacto:*

Nancy Gutierrez, Directora de Comunicación, DCYF | 360-742-6418 |nancy.gutierrez@dcyf.wa.gov
